9741c0b2-a22d-43aa-ae20-ec23b0e86c5c.text:
Efﬁcient Transfer Learning Schemes for Personalized Language Modeling using Recurrent Neural Network

Seunghyun Yoon 1,2 , Hyeongu Yun 1 , Yuna Kim 2 , Gyu-tae Park 2 and Kyomin Jung 1,3
1 Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Seoul National University, Seoul, Korea
2 Software R&D Center, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., Seoul, Korea


3 Automation and Systems Research Institute,

03d6a771-c218-4ac9-b965-4a9ba846df95.label: text_chunk
03d6a771-c218-4ac9-b965-4a9ba846df95.text:
Seoul National University, Seoul, Korea { mysmilesh, kjung } @snu.ac.kr, hyeongu.yun.1989@gmail.com, { yuna1.kim, gyutae.park } @samsung.com

Abstract

In this paper, we propose an efficient transfer leaning meth- ods for training a personalized language model using a re- current neural network with long short-term memory archi- tecture.

525d51fe-a836-4c9b-8d89-d2d963b071d7.label: text_chunk
525d51fe-a836-4c9b-8d89-d2d963b071d7.text: With our proposed fast transfer learning schemes, a general language model is updated to a personalized language model with a small amount of user data and a limited com- puting resource. These methods are especially useful for a mobile device environment while the data is prevented from transferring out of the device for privacy purposes.

e35a8539-bb34-4814-a8ee-70cd832759da.label: text_chunk
e35a8539-bb34-4814-a8ee-70cd832759da.text:
Through experiments on dialogue data in a drama, it is verified that our transfer learning methods have successfully generated the personalized language model, whose output is more similar to the personal language style in both qualitative and quantita- tive aspects.

Introduction

Recently there has been a considerable interest in language modeling due to various academic and commercial de- mands.

b9d868b2-1610-4336-bdbf-4091447187c7.label: text_chunk
b9d868b2-1610-4336-bdbf-4091447187c7.text: Academically, many studies have investigated this domain such as machine translation, chat-bot, message gen- eration, image tagging and other language-related areas. Commercially, it can be used as a core technology for pro- viding a new application on consumer products or services.

539c0ff4-9c78-4ea4-ab5e-a7efeda64dc3.label: text_chunk
539c0ff4-9c78-4ea4-ab5e-a7efeda64dc3.text: For instance, an automatic message-reply prediction service can be launched in mobile devices, thus helping a user to send a reply message when he/she is not provided with a proper input interface.To model the language of human dialogue, a recurrent neural network (RNN) structure is known to show the state of the arts performance with its ability to learn a sequen- tial pattern of the data (Sutskever, Vinyals, and Le 2014).

fcd0d1a2-a3a9-4ef6-8827-e0f786ad0d46.label: text_chunk
fcd0d1a2-a3a9-4ef6-8827-e0f786ad0d46.text: Among the RNN structures, a Long Short-Term Memory RNN (LSTM-RNN) and its variants are successfully used for language modeling tasks (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber 1997; Cho et al. 2014). However, as a kind of deep learning technique, the LSTM-RNN and the RNN structure requires both a large number of data and huge computing power to train the model properly.

005b1265-e4eb-4fee-b64b-80dff3fc0b68.label: text_chunk
005b1265-e4eb-4fee-b64b-80dff3fc0b68.text:
Hence any attempts for applying the RNN structure to personalized language modeling are mainly constrained by the following two limitations. First, personal mobile devices contain private message data among close acquaintances, so users seldom agree to transfer their log out of the devices. This causes a limitation of gathering
the whole user data to common computing spaces, where high-performance machines are available. Second, in rela- tively small computing machines, i.e.

05362688-a05e-4f02-93f2-f8bba1107d0f.label: text_chunk
05362688-a05e-4f02-93f2-f8bba1107d0f.text: , smart phone, it is not always-guaranteed to have enough resources to train a deep model within the devices.To resolve these limitations, we propose fast transfer learning schemes. It trains a base model with a large dataset and copies its first n-many layers to the first n-many layers of a target model. Then the target model is fine-tuned with relatively small target data.

a2c493e1-1423-4a51-a79c-c8773e87df8b.label: text_chunk
a2c493e1-1423-4a51-a79c-c8773e87df8b.text:
Several learning schemes such as freezing a certain layer or adding a surplus layer are pro- posed for achieving the result.

 In experiments, we trained a general language model with huge corpus such as an Work- shop on Statistical Machine Translation (WMT) data 1 and a movie script data by using powerful computing machines, and then transferred the model to target environment for up- dating to be a personalized language model.

908cac5a-b4a7-4035-88ba-3f97fabde41d.label: text_chunk
908cac5a-b4a7-4035-88ba-3f97fabde41d.text:
With this ap- proach, the final model can mimic target user’s language style with proper syntax.

In the experiments, we trained the general language model with literary-style data and applied the transfer learning with spoken-style data. Then we evaluated the model output for sentence completion task in a qualitative and a quantita- tive manner. The test result showed that the model learned the style of the target language properly.

204f04f7-154e-4c74-907f-c6b5a59823e6.label: text_chunk
204f04f7-154e-4c74-907f-c6b5a59823e6.text: Another test was conducted by training the general language model with the script of the drama, “Friends,” and by applying transfer learning with main character corpora from the script to gen- erate the personalized language model. The message-reply prediction task was evaluated with this model. The test result shows higher similarity between the output of the personal- ized language model and the same user dialogue than the one between the output of the personalized language model and other users’ dialogues.

8597897a-31c9-4ab6-9a99-1aab9ec7efeb.label: text_chunk
8597897a-31c9-4ab6-9a99-1aab9ec7efeb.text:
The contributions of this paper are as follows. First, we propose efficient transfer learning schemes for personalized language modeling, which is the first research on transfer learning for RNN based language models with privacy pre- serving. Second, we show the applicability of our research to the target scenario in the short message reply application by training the model in the similar environment to that of
1 Available from “http://www.statmt.org/wmt14/translation- task.html#download/”

da42bae1-ef12-459a-b062-8c5ca7582d7d.label: text_chunk
da42bae1-ef12-459a-b062-8c5ca7582d7d.text:
the mobile device, and highlight its test results.

Architecture for Personalized Language Model

As we are focusing on a personalized language modeling with the preservation of user data, we generate two types of language models. First is a sentence completion language model, which can complete sentences with a given n-many sequence of words. Second is a message-reply prediction language model, which can generate a response sentence for a given message.

dfe5a20a-ae91-4d65-92f4-47943fc18f4a.label: text_chunk
dfe5a20a-ae91-4d65-92f4-47943fc18f4a.text:
The output of both models implies user characteristics such as preferable vocabulary, sentence length, and other language-related patterns.

To achieve this result, we trained the language model with a large amount of general data in powerful computing en- vironments, and then applied the transfer learning in rela- tively small computing environments. We assume that this method would be applied to mobile devices.

2497c406-ead2-4f72-ae52-cb8d8c13f709.label: text_chunk
2497c406-ead2-4f72-ae52-cb8d8c13f709.text:
As we are tak- ing the preservation of privacy into consideration, the trans- ferred model is retrained within the local environments such as mobile devices, and no personal data is sent out of the devices.

 This could have been accomplished using the pro- posed transfer learning schemes in RNN-LSTM architec- ture.

Sentence Completion Language Model

A sentence completion model completes a sentence with the given word sequence X = { x 1 , x 2 , . .

Then 3 deep LSTM layers with 100 LSTM cells each and without peephole connection are used for learning the sequential pattern of the sentences.

Fast Transfer Learning Schemes

To generate a personalized language model with a small amount of user data and limited computing resources, trans- fer learning is essential. In the private data preservation sce- nario, we investigate three fast transfer learning schemes. Each scheme is described below:

• Scheme 1, relearn the whole layer: As a baseline,

 we re- train the whole model with private data only and compare the result with the two other schemes below.

983efb94-ab06-488a-82b5-c1b40dd04f9d.label: text_chunk
983efb94-ab06-488a-82b5-c1b40dd04f9d.text:
Because of the retraining of the LSTM layers in their entirety, this scheme requires more computing power than the other two schemes.

• Scheme 2, surplus layer: After the training of the model with general data,

 a surplus layer is inserted between the output layer and the last of the deep LSTM layers. Then, with private data, we update only the parameters of the surplus layer in the transfer learning phase.

7ae0b75f-8cf9-48db-b7a5-c2177caaa4cd.label: text_chunk
7ae0b75f-8cf9-48db-b7a5-c2177caaa4cd.text:
We assume that a user’s parlance could be modeled by learning addi- tional features in the user’s private data.

• Scheme 3, fixed first n layers: After training the model with general data,

 we fix the parameters in the first n LSTM layers (layer 1 and layer 2 in our experiments) and train remaining parameters in the transfer learning phase.

b74c9cd8-668a-43ea-8dd4-cc681d663985.label: text_chunk
b74c9cd8-668a-43ea-8dd4-cc681d663985.text:
We assume that the user’s parlance is a subset of the gen- eral pattern and the last layer plays the key role in deter- mining this pattern.

Measures

The perplexity is one of the popular measures for a lan- guage model. It measures how well the language model pre- dicts a sample. However, it is not good at measuring how well the output of the language model matches a target lan- guage style.

3c877451-d46d-4bce-8bc6-c3e57276d596.label: text_chunk
3c877451-d46d-4bce-8bc6-c3e57276d596.text: Another measure, the BLEU score algorithm (Papineni et al. 2002), has been widely used for the auto- matic evaluation of the model output.

 This ap- proach could be applied to a text evaluation where a large amount of data is available and certain words are used more frequently.

Datasets

• WMT14 ENG Corpus: The WMT’14 dataset includes several corpora.

e36805d9-faad-45c1-a165-59cdead187df.label: text_chunk
e36805d9-faad-45c1-a165-59cdead187df.text:
We only use an English part of the 10 9 French-English corpus. The dataset was crawled data from the bilingual web pages of the international orga- nizations (Callison-Burch et al. 2011). Thus, it contains high quality formal written language data. It consists of 21,000,000 sentences.

• English Bible Corpus: The English bible corpus is an- other type of written language data.

0941478b-c16c-4f0a-b51c-d8c88da42fd0.label: text_chunk
0941478b-c16c-4f0a-b51c-d8c88da42fd0.text:
It is useful data that differs from the WMT’14 dataset not only in the frequent vocabulary type but also in the average sentence length. It consists of 31,102 sentences.

• Drama Corpus: To collect spoken language data,

 we use drama data from “Friends” from opensubtitles 2 . We ex- tracted 69,000 sentences from dialogues, which we used to train a sentence completion language model.

e4b9b163-280a-4010-b845-b94b21771ecc.label: text_chunk
e4b9b163-280a-4010-b845-b94b21771ecc.text:
For the message-reply prediction language model, pairwise data is required. Among the extracted data, two consecutive sentences of different characters are linked into a single sentence to generate pairwise data.

• Main Character Corpora: From the drama corpus,

 we extract main character corpora to model personal users.

64d35e63-2326-4536-b488-c2d56d4d8808.label: text_chunk
64d35e63-2326-4536-b488-c2d56d4d8808.text:
For example, the Chanlder (one of the main characters in “Friends”) corpus consisted of 8,406 lines and the Rachel (another major character in “Friends”) corpus consisted of 9,194 lines. The former data could represent a male adult, and the latter data could represent a female adult. We assume that those amounts of data could be gathered in a user device for the personalizing language model.

Experiments

We mainly conduct two types of experiments.

With this setting, the difference between general and personalized language models can be measured in a quantitative and a qualitative manner.

Literary-Style to Spoken-Style Sentence Completion

We train a general language model of literary-style with the WMT’14 corpus. We then apply a transfer learning scheme with “Friends” drama data for the model to learn the spoken- style language.

General-Style to Personal-Style Message-Reply Prediction

We simulate the message-reply prediction scenario using the drama corpus. The script of the drama, “Friends,” is used to train a general language model, and two main character cor- pora are used to generate a personalized language model.

121dc933-4317-4122-996c-0c14bbec58f6.label: text_chunk
121dc933-4317-4122-996c-0c14bbec58f6.text:
For this message-reply prediction experiment, we use a vocabu- lary size of 18,107, and the out-of-vocabulary rate is about 3.5%.

 In the message-reply prediction case, pairwise data is generated by extracting the drama corpus of each charac- ter and concatenating two consecutive sentences of different characters to form one single message-reply sentence data.

8de6b61a-e01d-4bc8-9c2f-425a9ca0541a.text:
Related Work

Researchers have proposed language models using RNN, which learns the probability of next sequence data at the character or word level (Sutskever, Martens, and Hinton 2011; Graves 2013). The proposed language models were tested on web corpora (i.e. Wikipedia, news articles) and qualitative examples showed their applicability.

52d5788b-7697-4801-89bf-f71731b58725.label: text_chunk
52d5788b-7697-4801-89bf-f71731b58725.text: (Sutskever, Vinyals, and Le 2014) proposed a sequence-to-sequence learning algorithm with RNN and long short-term memory (LSTM) architecture (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber 1997), and (Cho et al. 2014) proposed RNN encoder-decoder ar- chitecture. Those studies were applied to the machine trans- lation problem.

46d0158a-3da7-495c-835c-701000a974b1.label: text_chunk

Those studies were similar to our research in the sense that both target message-reply predic- tion language model using RNN.

fd94392d-fe13-46de-b393-d923a55f8699.label: text_chunk
fd94392d-fe13-46de-b393-d923a55f8699.text: Our research, however, dif- fers in that it updates a general language model to a person- alized language model with user data separately, whereas the previous research trained a language model with the data, as a whole, in same place.In the commercial sphere, Google recently released a smart-reply service that could generate a response to a given email by using a sequence-to-sequence learning model (Google 2015).

f41ba073-5358-4592-9734-38867e4a99a6.label: text_chunk
f41ba073-5358-4592-9734-38867e4a99a6.text: There was another trial on the generation of responses in technical troubleshooting discourses (Vinyals and Le 2015). This research also required complete data in one place and did not provide a personalized model.Moreover, many researchers have conducted studies on transfer learning. (Bengio et al. 2011; Bengio 2012) sug- gested that a base-trained model with general data could be transferred to another domain. Recently, (Yosinski et al.

83875637-9316-40ad-bc10-0608b5873007.label: text_chunk
83875637-9316-40ad-bc10-0608b5873007.text: 2014) showed, through experiments, that the lower layers tended to have general features whereas the higher layer tended to have specific features. However, none of this re- search was applied to an RNN language model.To adapt a neural network model to an embedded system with limited resources, (Kim et al. 2015) (Han et al. 2015) reduced the size of the model by pruning the unnecessary connections within it.

6435051e-f237-467e-acf8-890516f63ba2.label: text_chunk
6435051e-f237-467e-acf8-890516f63ba2.text:
It repeatedly tried to reduce the model size without accuracy degradation. This research inspired us to a considerable extent. It applied a neural model to mo- bile devices. However, the research focused on reducing the model size using a powerful machine and releasing the final model to an embedded system, whereas ours investigated how to train a model within mobile devices so that private user data could be kept.

Conclusion

We propose an efficient method for training a personalized model using the LSTM-RNN model.

6301ff83-bc9c-472c-87c6-3232b39bcd26.label: text_chunk
6301ff83-bc9c-472c-87c6-3232b39bcd26.text:
To preserve users’ pri-
vacy, we suggest various transfer learning schemes so that the personalized language model can be generated within the user’s local environment. The proposed schemes “sur- plus layer’ and “fixed-n layer’ shows higher generalization performance whereas it trains only reduced number of pa- rameters than baseline model.

ad657c65-8c96-46c8-967e-3772528ee3f6.label: text_chunk
ad657c65-8c96-46c8-967e-3772528ee3f6.text: The quantitative and qualita- tive test result indicate that the output of the model is similar to that of the user’s style.It is certain that our proposed method reveals the appli- cability of the RNN-based language model in a user de- vice with the preservation of privacy. Furthermore, with our method the personalized language model can be generated with a smaller amount of user data than the huge amount of training data that is usually required in the traditional deep neural network discipline.

This approach seems likely to uncover enhanced learning schemes that require less data than was previously necessary.

community_summary.132:
**Summary of Relationships Involving MagiCoder**

1. **Testing and Evaluation**: MagiCoder has been rigorously tested on a dataset of approximately 4500 manually revised reports to assess its performance against human revisions. Its effectiveness is further evaluated using benchmarks and performance metrics such as PT, which measures error minimization and recall precision.

2. **Functionality and Purpose**: Designed to assist pharmacologists and pharmacovigilance stakeholders, MagiCoder streamlines the encoding of Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) reports, significantly reducing the time required for this task. It processes narrative descriptions of suspected ADRs and generates relevant MedDRA terms, enhancing the efficiency of report revisions.

3. **Technological Integration**: MagiCoder utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms to support pharmacovigilance activities and is implemented as a plug-in for VigiFarmaco, extending its functionality. It maps Italian text to the MedDRA dictionary and recognizes terms within the LLT (Lowest Level Terms) framework.

4. **Collaboration and Development**: The software is developed in collaboration with pharmacovigilance experts and is part of a set of voted solutions, indicating its relevance in the coding community. It also collaborates with human experts to improve the coding of adverse events.

5. **User Interaction**: MagiCoder is designed for direct interaction with users, requiring quick responses to queries. It assists human experts by providing tools to identify redundancy errors, thereby enhancing decision-making in data encoding.

6. **Performance and Limitations**: The performance of MagiCoder is influenced by the complexities of the Italian language, particularly due to synonymical groups that affect coding accuracy. It contrasts with part-of-speech methodologies, as it does not consider sentence structure in its analysis.

7. **Future Enhancements**: Suggestions for improvement include integrating a spell checker feature from VigiFarmaco to enhance user input accuracy and reduce errors. Additionally, MagiCoder aims to test its procedures against various medical classification systems, including ICD-9.

Overall, MagiCoder represents a significant advancement in the automation of pharmacovigilance processes, enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of ADR reporting and coding through advanced technology and collaborative development.

2. **Position Embeddings** are derived from **Character N-gram Embeddings**, suggesting that the latter serves as a foundational method for the computation of position embeddings. This indicates a hierarchical relationship where character n-gram embeddings provide the basis for developing position embeddings.
